summary: Gruber announces that sync is the next major feature coming to Vesper, his team's notes app, breaking their usual policy of not commenting on future plans. He explains why they spent the summer redesigning Vesper for iOS 7 rather than building new features, and defends their decision to ship 1.0 without sync. He acknowledges sync is the most requested feature and that many users consider it essential, while noting the technical difficulty of building it well. He humorously sets expectations at 'a year' while hoping to deliver sooner.
key_points: iOS 7 was such a fundamental platform change that Vesper needed a complete redesign, not a minor refresh, consuming the entire summer
Companies avoid announcing future plans because estimates feel like promises to customers, and optimism about timelines leads to disappointment
Shipping Vesper 1.0 without sync was a deliberate business decision that allowed them to operate profitably months earlier
Sync is notoriously difficult to solve — no option (iCloud, Dropbox, etc.) is simple from the inside, despite appearing so from the outside
Some users actually praised the lack of sync given NSA surveillance revelations, so syncing will always be optional and off by default
money_quotes: When you say what's coming next, people naturally want to know when. And when you tell them how long you think it will take, you're giving them a guess, but to the customer it feels like a promise.
In short, talking about work in progress and future plans is often a recipe for disappointing your customers.
We totally understand that for many of you, a notes app that doesn't sync to some sort of cloud storage service is a non-starter, full-stop.

From the outside, it may seem as simple as 'Just use _____', where the blank is iCloud or Dropbox or Azure or S3 or anything else. From the inside, every option we have presents hurdles, limitations, and a ton of work.

If I were to tell you what I honestly thought, I'd tell you I think it's going to take several months. But I don't want to disappoint you, so let's stick with 'a year' and hope that we can unveil it months ahead of schedule, instead of months behind.
Sync is the feature that almost everything else on our roadmap hinges upon.
We have no regrets at all about shipping Vesper 1.0 as an iPhone-only data silo.
key_insight: The tension between transparency and expectation management is real — companies stay silent about future plans not out of secrecy but because estimates become promises, and optimism about timelines inevitably disappoints.
